Historical Context: Lal Bagh Palace was the residence of the Holkar rulers of Indore. It was built in phases from 1886 to 1921 under Tukoji Rao Holkar II and Tukoji Rao III. The palace was modeled after Buckingham Palace.
Fun Facts:
The palace is spread over 28 acres of manicured gardens.
It has a stunning Durbar Hall with a crystal chandelier.
The palace now serves as a museum and government guest house.

Description: Named for its reddish stone steps, this ghat is popular with local residents.
Short Description: Red stone-stepped local ghat.
Historical Context: Named after the reddish laterite stone used in its construction.
Fun Facts: Popular with local Varanasi residents for daily bathing rituals.

Description: A complex of 11 monolithic cave churches carved from rock in Lalibela, Ethiopia.
Short Description: Rock-hewn churches of Lalibela, Ethiopia.
Historical Context: Built by King Lalibela as a New Jerusalem for pilgrims.
Fun Facts: UNESCO World Heritage Site. Still an active Christian pilgrimage destination.

Description: The freestanding bell tower of Pisa Cathedral, famous for its unintended tilt.
Short Description: Famous leaning bell tower in Pisa, Italy.
Historical Context: Tilt began during construction due to soft unstable soil.
Fun Facts: Tilt is about 3.97 degrees. Galileo allegedly dropped cannonballs from here.

Description: Leh is the capital of Ladakh, a high-altitude desert region in the Himalayas. It is home to spectacular Buddhist monasteries including Hemis, Thiksey, and Diskit, dramatic landscapes, and adventurous treks and bike rides.
Short Description: High-altitude Himalayan desert with spectacular Buddhist monasteries and biking routes.
Historical Context: Ladakh was an independent Buddhist kingdom for centuries before acceding to India in 1947. The Hemis monastery is the richest and one of the oldest in Ladakh. The Leh-Manali Highway and Leh-Srinagar Highway are among the world's highest motorable roads.
Fun Facts:
Ladakh has the world's highest motorable roads and passes.
The annual Hemis Festival features sacred Cham (mask) dances.
The Pangong Lake at 4,350 metres altitude changes colour throughout the day.

Description: A Baháʼí House of Worship in New Delhi, India. It is known for its unique lotus-like shape
Short Description: A Baháʼí House of Worship with a unique lotus-like shape
Historical Context: The temple is open to all faiths and is a symbol of peace and unity.
Fun Facts: The temple is made of white marble

Description: The Louvre Museum in Paris is the world's largest art museum and a historic monument. It houses over 380,000 objects including the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and the Winged Victory of Samothrace, attracting over 9 million visitors annually.
Short Description: World's largest art museum — home to the Mona Lisa and 380,000 artworks.
Historical Context: The Louvre was originally built as a fortress in the 12th century, then became a royal palace. It was converted into a museum during the French Revolution in 1793. The iconic glass pyramid entrance was added by I.M. Pei in 1989.
Fun Facts:
The Mona Lisa is much smaller than most visitors expect (77 x 53 cm).
It would take 100 days to see every piece of art if you spent 30 seconds each.
The Louvre was used as a royal palace for 400 years before becoming a museum.

Description: Lower Lake (Chhota Talab) is a smaller lake adjacent to the Upper Lake in Bhopal, connected by the Bridge of Boat. It is surrounded by gardens and offers boating facilities, making it a popular leisure spot.
Short Description: Smaller lake connected to Upper Lake — popular leisure and boating destination.
Historical Context: Lower Lake was created in 1794 during the reign of Nawab Hayat Muhammad Khan Bahadur. The lake is connected to Upper Lake through an undersea channel. Kamla Park on its eastern bank is one of the most popular recreational areas.
Fun Facts:
The lake is connected to Upper Lake through a natural channel.
Boating is available with pedal boats and rowboats for hire.
Kamla Park gardens along the lake are beautifully maintained.

Description: An Incan citadel set high in the Andes Mountains of Peru, built in the 15th century.
Short Description: Ancient Incan citadel in the Andes, Peru.
Historical Context: Built at the height of the Inca Empire, abandoned during the Spanish conquest.
Fun Facts: Rediscovered in 1911. No mortar was used in its stone construction.

Description: The Mahabodhi Temple Complex at Bodh Gaya is one of the four holy sites related to the life of Lord Buddha. The temple marks the location where Siddhartha Gautama, the Buddha, attained enlightenment. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Short Description: UNESCO World Heritage temple marking Buddha's enlightenment site.
Historical Context: Emperor Ashoka first built a temple at this site in the 3rd century BCE. The current temple was largely rebuilt by the Burmese in the 11th century. It was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2002 and is one of the most important pilgrimage sites for Buddhists worldwide.
Fun Facts:
The main tower of the temple is 55 metres tall.
A descendant of the original Bodhi Tree grows beside the temple.
Buddhists from over 50 countries have built monasteries in Bodh Gaya.

Description: Mahakal Lok (Mahakal Corridor) is a massive ₹856 crore project developed to enhance the pilgrimage experience at the Mahakaleshwar Temple. It features stunning sculptures, fountains, and a beautifully landscaped walkway.
Short Description: Massive ₹856 crore spiritual corridor with stunning sculptures leading to Mahakaleshwar Temple.
Historical Context: Mahakal Lok was inaugurated by Prime Minister Narendra Modi in 2022. The corridor spans 900 metres and features 108 pillars, 93 murals depicting scenes from Shiva Purana, and 200 sculptures. It has transformed the pilgrimage experience at Ujjain.
Fun Facts:
108 pillars line the 900-metre corridor.
200 sculptures depicting Shiva's stories line the walkway.
The project has increased pilgrim capacity from 10,000 to 1,00,000 per day.

Description: Mahakaleshwar Temple is one of the 12 Jyotirlingas of Lord Shiva, located in Ujjain, Madhya Pradesh. It is one of the most sacred pilgrimage sites in Hinduism. The unique Bhasma Aarti (ash ritual) performed here is a major attraction.
Short Description: One of the 12 sacred Jyotirlingas of Lord Shiva — most important temple of Ujjain.
Historical Context: Mahakaleshwar is considered the lord of time (Mahakal) and time passes under his rule. The temple's Shivalinga is a Swayambhu (self-manifested) one. The famous Bhasma Aarti at 4 AM uses sacred ash for worship.
Fun Facts:
It is one of only 12 Jyotirlingas — the most sacred Shiva shrines.
The 4 AM Bhasma Aarti is the most spectacular ritual in India.
Over 1.5 crore devotees visit during Kumbh Mela held here every 12 years.

Description: The lowest-lying nation on Earth, famous for crystal-clear waters, overwater bungalows, and coral reefs.
Short Description: Lowest nation Earth overwater bungalows coral reefs.
Historical Context: Comprised of 26 coral atolls and over 1,000 coral islands; threatened by rising sea levels.
Fun Facts:
The highest point in the Maldives is only 2.4 meters above sea level.
Average sea depth around the islands is just 1-2 meters.

Description: Features a historic astronomical observatory built by Maharaja Jai Singh II of Jaipur.
Short Description: Ghat with historic Jai Singh observatory.
Historical Context: Maharaja Jai Singh II built an astronomical observatory here in 1710 AD.
Fun Facts: Has an observatory identical to Jantar Mantar in Jaipur.

Description: Mangalnath Temple in Ujjain is dedicated to Mars (Mangal) planet, and Ujjain is considered the birthplace of the planet Mars according to Hindu astrology. The temple is especially important for people born under the Mangal dosha.
Short Description: Temple of planet Mars — Ujjain is considered Mars's birthplace in Hindu astrology.
Historical Context: According to the Matsya Purana, Ujjain is the birthplace of Mars. People born under Mangal Dosha (astrological affliction of Mars) visit this temple to perform remedies. Tuesdays are especially important as Tuesday is dedicated to Mars.
Fun Facts:
Tuesday is the most auspicious day to visit.
Ujjain is considered the birthplace of planet Mars.
The temple sits on the bank of the Shipra River with a beautiful setting.

Description: One of the most sacred ghats in Varanasi, known as the main cremation ghat. Fires here are said to have burned continuously for centuries.
Short Description: The eternal burning ghat of Varanasi.
Historical Context: According to Hindu belief, those cremated here achieve moksha (liberation).
Fun Facts:
Fires said to burn for over 3,500 years.
Around 100 cremations happen daily.

Description: Named after the sacred Lake Mansarovar in Tibet, this ghat is spiritually significant.
Short Description: Sacred Mansarovar lake-named ghat.
Historical Context: Named after the sacred Mansarovar Lake; symbolically connects Varanasi to Kailash-Mansarovar.
Fun Facts: Considered spiritually equivalent to bathing in Mansarovar.

Description: Manua Bhan Ki Tekri is a Jain pilgrimage hill in Bhopal, topped by a magnificent Jain temple. The hill offers panoramic views of Bhopal city and the Upper Lake. It is an important Jain religious site.
Short Description: Jain pilgrimage hill with temple offering panoramic views of Bhopal.
Historical Context: The hilltop temple is dedicated to the Jain Tirthankara Mahavira. The hill is sacred to the Jain community and has beautiful marble sculptures and a serene garden. The view from the top encompasses the entire city of Bhopal including both lakes.
Fun Facts:
The hilltop offers the best panoramic view of Bhopal city.
The marble Jain temple at the top is beautifully carved.
The hill is a popular spot for sunrise and sunset photography.

Description: Marrakech's ancient medina (old city) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, a labyrinth of souks, riads, and historic monuments. The main square Djemaa el-Fna is listed by UNESCO as an Intangible Cultural Heritage.
Short Description: UNESCO medina — a sensory explosion of souks, spices, and snake charmers.
Historical Context: Marrakech was founded in 1062 as the capital of the Almoravid dynasty. The Djemaa el-Fna square transforms every evening into a spectacle of storytellers, musicians, snake charmers, and food stalls. The medina's labyrinthine streets can take days to explore fully.
Fun Facts:
The Djemaa el-Fna square is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.
The medina has over 90,000 shops in its souks.
Marrakech is called the Red City for its distinctive pink-red sandstone buildings.

Description: The Grand Mosque in Mecca — holiest site in Islam, largest mosque in the world.
Short Description: Holiest mosque in Islam, Mecca.
Historical Context: Surrounds the Kaaba, considered the house of God in Islam.
Fun Facts: Accommodates over 2 million worshippers during Hajj.

Description: Meenakshi Amman Temple is a historic Hindu temple in Madurai, Tamil Nadu, dedicated to Goddess Meenakshi and Lord Sundareswarar. Its spectacular gopurams (towers) with thousands of colorful sculptures make it one of India's most magnificent temples.
Short Description: Ancient temple with spectacular colorful gopurams — Madurai's architectural crown.
Historical Context: The original temple was built by the legendary Pandya king Kulasekara Pandya. The current structure was largely built in the 17th century. The temple complex has 14 gopurams, the tallest being 52 metres. The temple receives 15,000-20,000 visitors daily.
Fun Facts:
The temple has 14 towers and 33,000 sculptured figures.
15,000-20,000 devotees visit daily — one of India's busiest temples.
The ancient 'Golden Lotus Tank' inside the temple is where poets tested their works.

Description: One of the most scenic ghats popular with tourists for boat rides.
Short Description: Scenic boat-ride tourist favourite ghat.
Historical Context: Known for its picturesque location and boat ride starting point.
Fun Facts: Great place to watch the sunrise over the Ganga.

Description: Meghdoot Garden is one of the most beautiful and well-maintained gardens in Indore. It is a popular recreational spot for families and features a lake, musical fountain, and a variety of plant species.
Short Description: Beautiful garden with lake, musical fountain, and children's activities.
Historical Context: Meghdoot Garden is named after Kalidasa's famous poem 'Meghdoot'. The garden has a beautiful lake at its center with boating facilities. The musical fountain show in the evenings draws large crowds.
Fun Facts:
The garden has a beautiful lake with paddle boat rides.
The musical fountain show runs every evening.
The garden is lit beautifully after sunset.

Description: Eastern Orthodox monasteries built atop natural sandstone rock pillars in Greece.
Short Description: Orthodox monasteries atop rock pillars, Greece.
Historical Context: Monks chose inaccessible peaks to escape persecution.
Fun Facts: UNESCO World Heritage Site. James Bond For Your Eyes Only filmed here.

Description: A breathtaking fjord in Fiordland National Park, New Zealand, often called the eighth wonder of the world.
Short Description: Eighth wonder world-famous New Zealand fjord.
Historical Context: Carved by glaciers during the ice ages; Mitre Peak rises 1,692m straight from the water.
Fun Facts:
Rainfall here averages 7 meters per year, creating hundreds of temporary waterfalls.
Fiordland has no roads – accessible only by boat or air.

Description: One of the major ghats with a striking view; popular for boat rides and sunrise photography.
Short Description: Major ghat famous for sunrise views.
Historical Context: An important ghat on the main riverside stretch, used by both locals and tourists.
Fun Facts: Offers stunning panoramic views of the ghats.

Description: A tidal island in Normandy topped with a stunning medieval abbey.
Short Description: Medieval island abbey in Normandy, France.
Historical Context: Used as a prison during the French Revolution.
Fun Facts: The island is cut off during high tide. Attracts 3 million visitors annually.

Description: A beautiful mosque built by Sikander Begum of Bhopal, inspired by the Jama Masjid of Delhi.
Short Description: Jama Masjid-inspired Sikander Begum mosque.
Historical Context: Built by the Nawab Begum Sikander; modeled on Jama Masjid in Delhi.
Fun Facts: The mosque's white marble domes give it a pearl-like appearance.

Description: Mount Everest is the world's highest mountain at 8,848.86 metres, located in the Himalayas on the Nepal-China border. The Everest Base Camp Trek is one of the world's most iconic and challenging treks.
Short Description: World's highest mountain — Everest Base Camp Trek is a bucket-list experience.
Historical Context: Everest was first summited by Sir Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay Sherpa on May 29, 1953. The mountain is called 'Sagarmatha' in Nepali and 'Chomolungma' in Tibetan, both meaning 'Goddess of the Sky'. Over 6,000 people have summited it to date.
Fun Facts:
The summit is 8,848.86 metres — the highest point on Earth.
Over 300 people have died attempting to climb Everest.
Over 6,000 people have successfully summited as of 2024.

Description: Mount Fuji is Japan's highest mountain at 3,776 metres and an active volcano. It is Japan's most iconic symbol, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and has inspired artists for centuries including Hokusai's famous 36 Views of Mount Fuji.
Short Description: Japan's iconic sacred mountain — highest peak and UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Historical Context: Mount Fuji last erupted in 1707-08. It is one of Japan's Three Holy Mountains and has been a site of Shinto and Buddhist pilgrimage for centuries. The mountain is visible from Tokyo on a clear day, 100 km away.
Fun Facts:
Over 200,000 people climb Mount Fuji every summer season.
The iconic 'Red Fuji' painting is by Hokusai.
Fuji has been a UNESCO Cultural Site since 2013.

Description: Munnar is a hill station in Kerala's Western Ghats, famous for its breathtaking rolling tea gardens, misty mountains, and cool climate. It is home to some of India's finest tea estates and rare wildlife including Nilgiri tahr.
Short Description: Kerala's most beautiful hill station — emerald tea gardens and misty Western Ghats.
Historical Context: Tea was introduced to Munnar by the British in the 1880s. The Tata Tea estates here produce some of India's finest tea. The Eravikulam National Park near Munnar is home to the largest population of the endangered Nilgiri tahr mountain goat.
Fun Facts:
Munnar is India's largest tea-growing area.
Eravikulam National Park has the world's largest Nilgiri tahr population.
The rolling tea gardens create one of India's most photogenic landscapes.

Description: A beautifully maintained ghat popular with photographers and tourists for its picturesque setting.
Short Description: Photogenic ghat popular with photographers.
Historical Context: Built by a local zamindar; features beautiful riverside architecture.
Fun Facts: A favourite of professional and amateur photographers.

Description: Mysore Dasara is one of India's grandest royal festivals, celebrated for 10 days during Navratri in Mysore. The Royal Mysore Palace is illuminated with 100,000 lights and the procession features decorated elephants.
Short Description: India's grandest royal festival — Mysore Palace lit with 100,000 lights for Dasara.
Historical Context: Mysore Dasara has been celebrated for over 400 years, even during the Wadiyar dynasty's rule. The main procession (Jamboo Savari) features a golden howdah carrying the goddess, mounted on a decorated elephant, followed by thousands of marching troops.
Fun Facts:
100,000 light bulbs illuminate Mysore Palace during Dasara.
The golden howdah (elephant saddle) weighs 750 kg.
Over 5 lakh people attend the grand final procession.

Description: The official residence and seat of the Kingdom of Mysore and the Wadiyar dynasty. It is a historical palace and a royal residence in Mysore, India
Short Description: Royal palace and official residence of the Wadiyar dynasty
Historical Context: Built in Indo-Saracenic style, it is one of the most famous tourist attractions in India.
Fun Facts:
Illuminated on Sundays and festivals.
Has over 90,000 light bulbs.
Built in Indo-Saracenic style.
Attracts millions of visitors annually

Description: Nalanda was an ancient Mahavihara — a large Buddhist monastery and centre of learning — located in Rajgir, Bihar, India. It was one of the greatest centres of learning in the ancient world, attracting scholars from across Asia.
Short Description: Ruins of the world's first residential university and ancient Buddhist learning centre.
Historical Context: Nalanda University flourished from the 5th to the 12th century CE and attracted students and scholars from China, Korea, Japan, Tibet, Mongolia, Turkey, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia. It was destroyed by the Khilji dynasty in 1193 CE. It was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2016.
Fun Facts:
It had a library of 9 million manuscripts called Dharmaganja.
It housed over 10,000 students and 2,000 teachers at its peak.
Hsuan Tsang, the famous Chinese traveller, studied here in the 7th century.

Description: Namo Ghat is a newly developed world-class ghat built in Varanasi as part of the Smart City Mission. It is a modern, well-designed space offering panoramic views of the Ganges and the ghats.
Short Description: Modern world-class ghat built under the Smart City Mission.
Historical Context: Namo Ghat was developed as part of the Varanasi Smart City project and inaugurated in 2021. It offers state-of-the-art facilities while maintaining the spiritual character of the ghats.
Fun Facts:
It is the newest and most modern ghat in Varanasi.
The ghat has a grand amphitheatre for cultural events.
It offers a 360° view of the Ganga and the ancient ghats nearby.

Description: A peaceful ghat with a Shiva temple, frequented by local devotees.
Short Description: Peaceful Shiva devotee local ghat.
Historical Context: Named after Nandeshwara, a form of Lord Shiva.
Fun Facts: Peaceful atmosphere ideal for meditation and prayer.

Description: Named after the sage Narada, this ghat features beautiful sculptures and is known for its peaceful atmosphere.
Short Description: Serene ghat named after sage Narada.
Historical Context: Named after Narada Muni, the divine sage of Hindu mythology.
Fun Facts: Famous for its beautiful stone carvings and peaceful ambiance.

Description: A newer ghat with modern facilities, popular among tourists.
Short Description: Modern facility ghat for tourists.
Historical Context: One of the more recently developed ghats with better accessibility.
Fun Facts: Good starting point for guided ghat tours.

Description: Neuschwanstein Castle is a 19th-century Romanesque Revival palace in Bavaria, Germany, commissioned by King Ludwig II. It inspired Walt Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle and is one of Germany's most visited monuments.
Short Description: Fairy-tale castle that inspired Disney's Sleeping Beauty — 1.4 million visitors annually.
Historical Context: King Ludwig II began construction in 1869 but died in 1886 before it was completed. He lived in the castle for only 172 days before his mysterious death. Disney's Imagineers used it as the model for Sleeping Beauty's Castle in Disneyland.
Fun Facts:
It inspired Walt Disney's Sleeping Beauty and Cinderella castles.
King Ludwig II lived here for only 172 days before his death.
The castle was opened to the public just 7 weeks after the king's death.

Description: The Ngorongoro Crater is the world's largest intact volcanic caldera and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It shelters the highest density of wildlife in Africa including all of the Big Five in a self-contained ecosystem.
Short Description: World's largest intact volcanic caldera — Africa's most concentrated wildlife area.
Historical Context: Ngorongoro Crater was formed about 2.5 million years ago when a massive volcano collapsed. The 610 sq km caldera floor is home to 25,000 large animals including the densest population of lions in Africa.
Fun Facts:
The crater floor is home to around 25,000 large mammals.
It has the densest lion population in Africa.
Masai people are permitted to graze their cattle in the conservation area.

Description: Niagara Falls is a group of three waterfalls at the southern end of Niagara Gorge on the border between Canada and USA. The falls are the world's most powerful waterfall by flow rate and one of the most visited natural attractions in the world.
Short Description: World's most powerful waterfall by flow rate — on the Canada-USA border.
Ticket Price: Free to view (Maid of Mist cruise: CAD 30)
Historical Context: Niagara Falls was formed about 10,000 years ago during the last Ice Age. The three waterfalls (Horseshoe, American, Bridal Veil) carry 90% of North America's fresh water drainage. The falls have been a honeymoon destination since the 1800s.
Fun Facts:
Niagara Falls is the world's most powerful waterfall by flow rate.
Over 12 million people visit every year.
The falls move upstream about 30 cm each year due to erosion.

Description: A sacred ghat in Varanasi on the banks of the Ganges, associated with the Nishad community.
Short Description: Sacred Ghat of the Nishad community, Varanasi.
Historical Context: Associated with the boatman Nishad who aided Lord Rama in the Ramayana.
Fun Facts: Considered sacred by the Nishad boatman community of Varanasi.

Description: The Aurora Borealis visible from Iceland between September and March, one of nature's most spectacular displays.
Short Description: Spectacular Aurora Borealis natural light show Iceland.
Historical Context: Caused by solar particles interacting with Earth's atmosphere; Iceland is one of the best viewing locations.
Fun Facts:
The lights can appear in green, purple, pink and red.
Best seen away from city lights.

Description: The Norwegian fjords are UNESCO-listed natural wonders — steep-walled inlets carved by glaciers over millions of years. Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord are among the world's most spectacular landscapes.
Short Description: UNESCO Natural Heritage — glacier-carved fjords of breathtaking beauty.
Historical Context: Norwegian fjords were formed when glaciers carved deep valleys that were then flooded by the sea as glaciers retreated. Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord were designated UNESCO World Heritage Sites in 2005.
Fun Facts:
Geirangerfjord has Seven Sisters and Suitor waterfalls cascading into it.
The fjords reach depths of over 1,300 metres.
Norway has over 1,000 fjords along its coastline.

Description: Nubra Valley is a high-altitude cold desert in Ladakh, India, sitting at 3,048 metres. It is famous for its dramatic landscapes of sand dunes, double-humped Bactrian camels, ancient monasteries, and views of the Siachen Glacier.
Short Description: High-altitude desert with Bactrian camels and ancient monasteries in Ladakh.
Historical Context: Nubra Valley sits between the Karakoram and Ladakh mountain ranges. The valley was part of the ancient Silk Road and still has Bactrian (double-humped) camels that were used for trade. The Khardung La Pass (5,359 m) connecting Leh to Nubra was long claimed as the world's highest motorable road.
Fun Facts:
The valley has the world's highest sand dunes (at 3,000 m altitude).
Double-humped Bactrian camels are unique to this region in India.
The Khardung La Pass connecting to Leh is one of the world's highest roads.

Description: The world's largest inland delta, a UNESCO World Heritage Site teeming with wildlife in Botswana.
Short Description: UNESCO world's largest inland wildlife delta.
Historical Context: The Okavango River floods the delta each year, creating a temporary oasis in the Kalahari Desert.
Fun Facts:
One of the few remaining pristine ecosystems on Earth.
Home to the Big Five.

Description: A palace in the middle of a lake in Chittorgarh Fort, Rajasthan, India. It is associated with the story of Rani Padmini
Short Description: A palace in the middle of a lake
Historical Context: The palace is a symbol of the courage and sacrifice of Rani Padmini.
Fun Facts: o	The palace is surrounded by a beautiful lake

Description: Named after the mythical confluence of five rivers. A significant bathing ghat with historical Mughal connections.
Short Description: Mythical 5-river confluence ghat.
Historical Context: Aurangzeb built a mosque here; Tulsi Das is said to have composed the Ramcharitmanas nearby.
Fun Facts: One of the largest ghats in Varanasi.

Description: Panchkot Ghat is one of Varanasi's ancient bathing ghats on the banks of the Ganges. It is named after the five forts (Panch Kots) that historically protected the holy city and is a significant pilgrimage site.
Short Description: Ancient ghat named after the five fortifications that protected holy Varanasi.
Historical Context: Panchkot Ghat is associated with the ancient tradition of protecting Varanasi through five strategic fortifications. The ghat has been a site of religious bathing since antiquity and pilgrims come here throughout the year.
Fun Facts:
The ghat is one of Varanasi's quieter spots for meditation.
Boat rides from the ghat offer serene views of the cityscape.
The ghat hosts small-scale evening prayer rituals.

Description: A popular ghat for boat rides and sunrise viewing, frequented by tourists.
Short Description: Popular boat-ride and sunrise ghat.
Historical Context: One of the central ghats on the main tourist circuit.
Fun Facts: Great starting point for sunrise boat rides.

Description: The ancient temple atop the Acropolis dedicated to goddess Athena, built 432 BCE.
Short Description: Ancient temple of Athena, Athens.
Historical Context: Built under Pericles during the Golden Age of Athens.
Fun Facts: Columns lean slightly inward to create the optical illusion of straightness.

Description: Torres del Paine National Park in Chilean Patagonia is one of the world's most dramatic wilderness areas, featuring the iconic granite towers, glaciers, turquoise lakes, and diverse wildlife including pumas and condors.
Short Description: World's most dramatic wilderness — granite towers, glaciers, and Patagonian wildlife.
Historical Context: Torres del Paine was designated a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in 1978. The park's three famous granite towers (Las Torres) were formed by magma pushing through the earth's crust. The W-Trek and O-Trek are among the world's most famous hiking routes.
Fun Facts:
The famous W-Trek takes 4-5 days and is one of the world's best hikes.
Pumas inhabit the park and can sometimes be spotted at dawn.
The park's name means 'Blue Towers' in indigenous Tehuelche language.

Description: Patalpani Waterfall is a stunning 300-foot waterfall located 35 km from Indore in the Mhow area. It is one of Madhya Pradesh's most spectacular waterfalls, especially during and after monsoon.
Short Description: Spectacular 300-foot waterfall, the most popular natural attraction near Indore.
Historical Context: Patalpani is derived from the Hindi words meaning 'underground water'. The waterfall is formed by the Chambal River tributaries and plunges dramatically into a deep gorge. It is surrounded by lush forest.
Fun Facts:
The waterfall is most spectacular during and after monsoon (Jul-Sep).
A historic railway bridge near the falls is a popular photography spot.
The area is part of the Mhow-Mahow forest zone.

Description: Pattadakal is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Karnataka containing a remarkable group of 8th-century CE temples. It was the site of royal coronations of the Chalukya dynasty and showcases both northern and southern Indian architectural styles.
Short Description: UNESCO Heritage coronation site — 8th century Chalukya temples blending North and South styles.
Historical Context: Pattadakal served as the ceremonial capital of the Chalukya dynasty and was the site of royal coronations. The group of monuments demonstrates the transition in South Indian temple architecture from Vesara to Dravidian style.
Fun Facts:
The temples were built to commemorate victories over the Pallavas.
Both North Indian (Nagara) and South Indian (Dravidian) styles exist side by side.
The Virupaksha Temple is a precursor to the Brihadeshwara Temple at Thanjavur.

Description: The iconic rose-red carved Al-Khazneh (The Treasury) facade in Petra, Jordan.
Short Description: Rose-red carved Treasury of Petra, Jordan.
Historical Context: Capital of the Nabataean Kingdom, carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs.
Fun Facts: Known as the Rose City. Featured in Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade.

Description: The Petronas Twin Towers in Kuala Lumpur were the world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004. Standing at 451.9 metres, they are a symbol of Malaysia's economic rise and modern ambition.
Short Description: Iconic twin towers — once world's tallest buildings, symbol of Malaysia.
Historical Context: The Petronas Towers were designed by Argentine-American architect César Pelli. Each tower has 88 floors and is connected by a skybridge at floors 41-42. They remain the world's tallest twin towers.
Fun Facts:
The towers were the world's tallest buildings from 1998 to 2004.
They were built by two different companies (Samsung & Hazama) simultaneously.
The KLCC Park at the base is free and beautifully maintained.

Description: Pipliyapala Regional Park, also known as Atal Bihari Vajpayee Regional Park, is a beautiful lake and garden complex in Indore. It offers boat rides, children's activities, and evening light shows.
Short Description: Beautiful lake and garden complex with boat rides and light shows.
Historical Context: Pipliyapala Regional Park was developed by the Indore Municipal Corporation as a recreational space. The park has a large lake where paddle boats and motorboats are available for hire. Musical fountain and laser shows are held in the evenings.
Fun Facts:
The park has a stunning musical fountain that operates in evenings.
Boating facility is available for children and adults.
The park is illuminated beautifully at night.

Description: A UNESCO national park in Croatia featuring 16 cascading lakes and waterfalls.
Short Description: Stunning cascading lakes and waterfalls, Croatia.
Historical Context: Croatia's oldest and largest national park, established in 1949.
Fun Facts: Water colours change from turquoise to blue to green depending on minerals and light.

Description: A quiet and peaceful ghat used mainly by locals for bathing rituals.
Short Description: Peaceful local bathing ghat.
Historical Context: A relatively lesser-known ghat used primarily by local residents.
Fun Facts: Great spot for photography away from tourist crowds.

Description: Prague's Old Town (Staré Město) is one of Europe's best-preserved medieval cities, centered around the Old Town Square with the famous Astronomical Clock. The city's Gothic and Baroque architecture earned it UNESCO Heritage status.
Short Description: UNESCO medieval city — Gothic architecture and Astronomical Clock of Prague.
Historical Context: Prague's Old Town Square has been the city's market since the 10th century. The Astronomical Clock (Orloj), installed in 1410, is the third oldest in the world and still functioning. Prague survived World War II largely intact, preserving its medieval character.
Fun Facts:
Prague's Astronomical Clock has been working since 1410.
The city was never heavily bombed in WWII, preserving its medieval character.
Kafka, Mozart, and Einstein all lived in Prague at various times.

Description: Named after the devotee Prahlad, this ghat has a temple and is popular with Vaishnavites.
Short Description: Devotee Prahlad-dedicated Vaishnava ghat.
Historical Context: Named after the legendary Vaishnava devotee Prahlad from Hindu mythology.
Fun Facts: Known for Vaishnavite religious practices.

Description: The Pyramids of Giza are the only surviving wonder of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Built as tombs for pharaohs over 4,500 years ago, they remain one of humanity's greatest architectural achievements.
Short Description: Only surviving Ancient Wonder — 4,500-year-old pharaoh tombs and the Great Sphinx.
Historical Context: The Great Pyramid of Khufu was the world's tallest man-made structure for 3,800 years. The pyramid is estimated to contain 2.3 million stone blocks, each weighing 2.5 to 15 tonnes. The precision of alignment is so exact that a credit card cannot be inserted between the stones.
Fun Facts:
The Great Pyramid was the world's tallest structure for 3,800 years.
2.3 million stone blocks averaging 2.5 tonnes each were used.
The pyramid is aligned to true north with 0.05 degree accuracy — more precise than modern structures.

Description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site in Delhi, India. It is a minaret that is the tallest brick minaret in the world
Short Description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and the tallest brick minaret in the world
Historical Context: The minaret was built by Qutb-ud-din Aibak
Fun Facts:
The minaret has five stories.
It is a symbol of Indo-Islamic architecture.

Description: Located near the old railway bridge, this ghat is associated with ancient trade routes along the Ganga.
Short Description: Ancient trade-route ghat near railway bridge.
Historical Context: This ghat was a significant port during the ancient and medieval periods.
Fun Facts: Archaeological excavations here revealed ancient Varanasi civilization.

Description: Named after India's first President Dr. Rajendra Prasad, a modern and well-maintained ghat.
Short Description: Ghat named after India's first President.
Historical Context: Named in honor of Dr. Rajendra Prasad, the first President of independent India.
Fun Facts: One of the more recently renovated ghats with modern facilities.

Description: Rajwada is a historic seven-storey palace built in the 19th century by the Holkar dynasty, rulers of Indore. It is a magnificent blend of Mughal, French, and Maratha architectural styles.
Short Description: Historic 7-storey Holkar dynasty palace in the heart of Indore.
Historical Context: Rajwada was the royal palace of the Holkar dynasty who ruled Indore. The original structure was built by Malhar Rao Holkar. It was burnt three times and rebuilt — the current structure dates to the 19th century.
Fun Facts:
The palace is 7 stories tall with the upper floors in wood.
Light and Sound show is held here on special occasions.
The clock tower within the complex is an iconic landmark.

Description: Ralamandal Wildlife Sanctuary is a small forest sanctuary located 12 km from Indore. It is home to deer, nilgai, and various bird species and serves as a green lung for the city of Indore.
Short Description: Small forest sanctuary 12 km from Indore with deer and diverse birdlife.
Historical Context: Ralamandal was declared a wildlife sanctuary in 1989. The forest covers an area of 2.34 sq km and has a heritage structure — the old Holkar hunting lodge — at its center. It offers nature trails and wildlife viewing.
Fun Facts:
The sanctuary has spotted deer, nilgai, and over 100 bird species.
The old Holkar hunting lodge inside is a heritage structure.
A herbal garden inside the sanctuary has 150+ medicinal plants.

Description: Ram Ghat is the most sacred bathing ghat in Ujjain on the banks of the Shipra River. It is the site of the main bathing ritual during Kumbh Mela and hosts a beautiful evening Ganga Aarti daily.
Short Description: Most sacred bathing ghat in Ujjain, site of Kumbh Mela's main bathing ritual.
Historical Context: Ram Ghat is considered the most important of the 23 ghats in Ujjain. According to legend, Lord Rama bathed here while on his way to Lanka. The evening aarti on the Shipra River is a spiritual spectacle.
Fun Facts:
The evening Shipra Aarti here rivals the famous Ganga Aarti of Varanasi.
The main Kumbh Mela bathing ritual (Shahi Snan) occurs here.
Boat rides on the Shipra River offer panoramic ghat views.

Description: A ghat with a large royal mansion built by the rulers of a Rajput clan.
Short Description: Rajput ruler's royal mansion ghat.
Historical Context: Built by Rajput rulers; the Rana Mahal palace dominates the riverside.
Fun Facts: Offers fine examples of Rajput architecture.

Description: The Rann of Kutch is a vast salt marsh in Gujarat, India. During winter, the white salt crust stretches to the horizon and is the venue of the spectacular Rann Utsav festival under a full moon.
Short Description: Vast white salt desert of Gujarat — spectacular Rann Utsav festival destination.
Historical Context: The Rann of Kutch is one of the world's largest salt deserts, covering 30,000 sq km. It turns into a shallow sea during monsoon and a brilliant white salt flat in winter. The Rann Utsav festival is held between November and February.
Fun Facts:
The White Rann glows silver under a full moon — a magical sight.
Flamingos nest in the Great Rann in millions.
The Rann Utsav festival has folk music, crafts, and cultural events.

Description: A renowned wildlife reserve and tiger reserve in Rajasthan, India, built around ancient ruins.
Short Description: Tiger reserve with ancient ruins, Rajasthan.
Historical Context: Named after the 10th-century Ranthambore Fort that stands within the park.
Fun Facts: One of the best places in India to spot Bengal tigers in the wild.

Description: Ravidas Ghat is named after the 15th-century saint-poet Ravidas, a revered Bhakti movement saint. It is an important pilgrimage site for the Ravidassia community and followers of saint Ravidas.
Short Description: Ghat named after the great 15th-century Bhakti saint Ravidas.
Historical Context: Sant Ravidas was a mystic poet-saint of the Bhakti movement born in Varanasi in the 15th century. His teachings emphasized devotion to God irrespective of caste or religion.
Fun Facts:
Sant Ravidas Jayanti is celebrated with great fervor here.
The ghat has a temple dedicated to Sant Ravidas.
Millions of devotees visit during the Ravidas Jayanti festival.

Description: An old and revered temple in Sehore district, known for its annual fair.
Short Description: Revered old temple with annual fair.
Historical Context: An ancient temple that hosts a large annual fair attracting pilgrims from across Madhya Pradesh.
Fun Facts: The annual mela (fair) is a major local event.

Description: Built by the rulers of Rewa state, featuring a distinctive palatial structure.
Short Description: Rewa royal state ghat.
Historical Context: Constructed by the maharajas of Rewa state in central India.
Fun Facts: Reflects the patronage of various Indian royal families.

Description: Rio de Janeiro is Brazil's most iconic city, famous for its stunning setting between mountains and the sea, the Christ the Redeemer statue, Copacabana and Ipanema beaches, and the world-famous Carnival.
Short Description: Brazil's most iconic city — beaches, samba, Christ the Redeemer, and Carnival.
Historical Context: Rio was Brazil's capital for 197 years until Brasília was founded in 1960. The city is famous for the Carnival, held 40 days before Easter, which draws 2 million people per day. Sugarloaf Mountain (Pão de Açúcar) and Christ the Redeemer frame the city's skyline.
Fun Facts:
Rio Carnival draws 2 million people per day to the streets.
Sugarloaf Mountain offers the city's most iconic views.
Ipanema and Copacabana are among the world's most famous beaches.

Description: The Ross Ice Shelf is the world's largest ice shelf, roughly the size of France, floating in Antarctica's Ross Sea. It serves as a platform for research stations and is the departure point for expeditions to the South Pole.
Short Description: World's largest ice shelf — size of France, gateway to South Pole expeditions.
Historical Context: The Ross Ice Shelf was discovered by explorer James Clark Ross in 1841. It is 487,000 sq km — roughly the size of France — and up to 750 metres thick. Robert Falcon Scott's and Roald Amundsen's expeditions to the South Pole began from the shelf.
Fun Facts:
Both Scott and Amundsen began their South Pole expeditions here.
The ice shelf is up to 750 metres thick.
The shelf is slowly calving massive icebergs into the ocean.

Description: Rotorua is New Zealand's geothermal wonderland, featuring geysers, boiling mud pools, hot springs, and Maori cultural experiences. Pohutu Geyser is the Southern Hemisphere's largest active geyser.
Short Description: New Zealand's geothermal wonderland — geysers, mud pools, and Maori culture.
Historical Context: Rotorua sits on the Taupo Volcanic Zone — one of the world's most active geothermal areas. The entire city smells of sulphur from the geothermal activity. Pohutu Geyser erupts up to 60 times daily, reaching 30 metres high.
Fun Facts:
Rotorua smells of sulphur throughout the city due to geothermal activity.
Pohutu Geyser erupts up to 60 times per day.
The local Maori culture is among the most accessible in New Zealand.

Description: La Sagrada Família is a monumental Catholic basilica in Barcelona designed by architect Antoni Gaudí. It has been under construction since 1882 and is expected to be completed in 2026 — a century after Gaudí's death.
Short Description: Gaudí's masterpiece basilica — under construction for over 140 years in Barcelona.
Historical Context: Antoni Gaudí took over the Sagrada Família project in 1883 and devoted the rest of his life to it. He died in 1926 in a tram accident, having completed only one tower. The project continues based on his plans and models.
Fun Facts:
The Sagrada Família has been under construction for over 140 years.
It is the most visited monument in Spain with 4.5 million visitors annually.
Gaudí is buried in its crypt — a pilgrimage site for Catholics.

Description: The world's largest hot desert covering most of northern Africa; Merzouga dunes in Morocco are iconic.
Short Description: World's largest hot desert iconic sand dunes.
Historical Context: The Sahara has been a desert for about 7 million years; sand dunes cover only 25% of its area.
Fun Facts:
Temperatures can swing from -4°C at night to 47°C by day.
Covers 11 countries.

Description: Sakka Ghat is one of the lesser-known but spiritually significant ghats of Varanasi on the banks of the Ganges. It is a peaceful ghat used for religious bathing and meditation.
Short Description: A serene and peaceful ghat for meditation and religious bathing.
Historical Context: Sakka Ghat has served as a spiritual retreat for ascetics and pilgrims for centuries. Its relatively quiet atmosphere makes it popular with those seeking solitude on the Ganga.
Fun Facts:
The ghat offers some of the most peaceful sunrise experiences.
Local fishermen use the ghat for their daily activities.
The ghat is a favorite spot for photography enthusiasts.

Description: The Atacama Desert is the driest non-polar desert on Earth, covering 105,000 sq km in northern Chile. The Salar de Atacama salt flat is home to flamingo colonies and the clearest night skies on Earth — making it the world's premier stargazing destination.
Short Description: World's driest desert — premier stargazing destination and flamingo habitat.
Historical Context: The Atacama has areas that have never recorded rainfall. Its extreme altitude (2,400 m average), dry air, and minimal light pollution make it the world's best location for astronomical observatories — over 60 observatories are planned or operational here.
Fun Facts:
Some parts of the Atacama have never recorded a single drop of rain.
The clearest night skies on Earth make it the world's top astronomy location.
Flamingos inhabit the salt flats despite the harsh conditions.

Description: The world's largest salt flat in Bolivia, creating a perfect mirror reflection of the sky when covered with water.
Short Description: World's largest salt flat sky-mirror Bolivia.
Historical Context: Formed from prehistoric lakes; contains over 70% of the world's lithium reserves.
Fun Facts:
The reflection is so perfect it's used to calibrate satellites.
Covers 10,582 sq km.

Description: Salkanpur is a hilltop temple dedicated to Goddess Bijasan Mata, located near Sehore in Madhya Pradesh. It is one of the most visited religious sites in the region, situated on a scenic hill.
Short Description: Hilltop temple of Bijasan Mata near Sehore — major pilgrimage site.
Historical Context: Salkanpur Devi Temple is dedicated to Goddess Bijasan Mata, a form of Goddess Durga. The hilltop location provides breathtaking views of the surrounding countryside. Thousands of devotees climb the hill during Navratri.
Fun Facts:
The temple is located 45 km from Bhopal and 28 km from Sehore.
Over 1 lakh devotees visit during Navratri festival.
A motorable road and steps lead to the temple at the hilltop.

Description: A serene ghat associated with Lord Shiva, offering peaceful river views.
Short Description: Serene Lord Shiva ghat.
Historical Context: One of the quieter ghats associated with Shiva worship.
Fun Facts: Less crowded; great for peaceful meditation by the river.

Description: Sanchi is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located 46 km from Bhopal. It contains some of the oldest Buddhist monuments in the world, including the Great Stupa built by Emperor Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE.
Short Description: UNESCO World Heritage Buddhist stupa built by Emperor Ashoka, near Bhopal.
Historical Context: Emperor Ashoka built the original stupa at Sanchi in the 3rd century BCE to house the relics of the Buddha. The famous carved gateways (toranas) were added in the 1st century BCE and are masterpieces of Indian art.
Fun Facts:
The four gateways at Sanchi are among the finest examples of Buddhist art.
The stupa was rediscovered by British General Taylor in 1818.
Sanchi is considered the birthplace of Buddhist art in India.

Description: Sandipani Ashram is believed to be the place where Lord Krishna, Balrama, and Sudama received their education from their guru Sandipani Muni. It is a sacred pilgrimage site for Krishna devotees.
Short Description: Sacred ashram where Lord Krishna received his education from Guru Sandipani.
Historical Context: According to Hindu scriptures, Lord Krishna and his brother Balrama came to Ujjain to study under the great sage Sandipani Muni. They are said to have mastered 64 arts in just 64 days. A stone tablet here is believed to be the original writing board used by Krishna.
Fun Facts:
A stone slab believed to be used by Krishna for writing still exists here.
The ashram has ancient stone inscriptions mentioning the Gurukul.
This is one of the few places directly associated with Krishna's education.

Description: The iconic Greek island with white-washed buildings and blue-domed churches perched on volcanic caldera cliffs.
Short Description: Iconic Greek island blue-dome white-washed volcanic cliffs.
Historical Context: The island is the remnant of a massive volcanic eruption around 1600 BCE.
Fun Facts:
The caldera is one of the world's largest active volcanic fields.
Sunset from Oia is one of the world's most photographed.

Description: Sarafa Bazaar is Indore's famous night food market, one of the most celebrated street food destinations in India. By day it is a jewelry market; by night it transforms into a legendary open-air food street.
Short Description: India's most famous night street food market, open daily after 9 PM.
Historical Context: Sarafa Bazaar has been a jewellers' market since ancient times. In the 1950s, food vendors began setting up after the jewelry shops closed. Today it is considered one of the best street food experiences in India.
Fun Facts:
Indore has won India's cleanest city award 7 times — partly due to Sarafa.
Must-try: Garadu, Bhutte ka Kees, Jalebi, and Shahi Shikanji.
The market sees over 15,000 visitors on weekends.

Description: A partially submerged ghat with a temple half-sunk into the Ganga, creating a mystical atmosphere.
Short Description: Half-submerged temple ghat.
Historical Context: Built by the Scindia rulers; the temple sank due to the weight of the structure.
Fun Facts: The sunken Shiva temple is a major photography spot.

Description: Historical fort in Sehore city, built during the medieval period, now partially in ruins but historically significant.
Short Description: Medieval historical fort in Sehore.
Historical Context: Built by local rulers during the medieval period; served as an administrative and military center.
Fun Facts: Remnants show the strategic importance of Sehore in central India.

Description: The Serengeti is Tanzania's most famous national park, renowned for the Great Migration — the annual movement of over 1.5 million wildebeest, zebras, and gazelles across the savanna. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Short Description: UNESCO Heritage park — home to the world's greatest wildlife migration.
Historical Context: The Serengeti's Great Migration is one of the world's most spectacular natural events. The wildebeest follow a clockwise circuit of about 800 km following the rains and grass. The Mara River crossings, where crocodiles ambush the herds, are particularly dramatic.
Fun Facts:
1.5 million wildebeest migrate in a clockwise circle annually.
The Mara River crossings are among nature's most dramatic scenes.
The Serengeti has the highest density of large mammals in Africa.

Description: Shaukat Mahal is a unique palace in Bhopal built in an Indo-Saracenic style blending Renaissance and European Gothic architecture. It was built in the 19th century and is a remarkable architectural curiosity.
Short Description: Unique palace blending Gothic, Renaissance, and Indian architectural styles.
Historical Context: Shaukat Mahal was built by a descendant of French royal family, Monsieur La Perron, who had converted to Islam. The palace blends French Gothic, Renaissance, and Indian architectural elements making it a truly unique structure.
Fun Facts:
The palace was designed by a French architect.
It blends French Gothic, Italian, and Mughal styles.
It stands adjacent to the Sadar Manzil and Jama Masjid of Bhopal.

Description: Dedicated to Goddess Sheetla, this ghat is visited by devotees seeking blessings for health.
Short Description: Goddess Sheetla healing blessings ghat.
Historical Context: Goddess Sheetla is worshipped for protection from diseases, especially smallpox.
Fun Facts: Special puja performed during Sheetla Ashtami festival.

Description: One of the sacred ghats on the Ganges in Varanasi dedicated to Goddess Shitala, the deity of smallpox and skin diseases. Pilgrims come to seek blessings for protection from diseases.
Short Description: Sacred ghat dedicated to Goddess Shitala in Varanasi.
Historical Context: Devotees worship Goddess Shitala here who is believed to cure skin diseases. The ghat has an ancient temple that draws pilgrims throughout the year.
Fun Facts:
The ghat is especially busy during Shitala Saptami festival.
Boat rides from here offer panoramic views of the ghats.
The ghat is one of the quieter ghats, offering a peaceful experience.

Description: Also known as Kali Ghat, features a large Shiva temple and is popular for boat rides.
Short Description: Shiva temple ghat popular for boat rides.
Historical Context: Built by the Maratha ruler Baji Rao II; houses an old Shiva temple.
Fun Facts: Offers great views of the river at sunrise.

Description: A scenic area near Sehore with natural beauty and small temples.
Short Description: Scenic area with temples near Sehore.
Historical Context: Popular picnic spot with views of the surrounding countryside.
Fun Facts: Ideal for nature walks and photography.

Description: Ancient rock fortress and palace ruin built by King Kashyapa in Sri Lanka.
Short Description: Ancient sky palace atop granite rock, Sri Lanka.
Historical Context: King Kashyapa chose this site for his fortified capital.
Fun Facts: Called the Eighth Wonder of the World. Ancient frescoes still visible.

Description: A steep rocky island off the Kerry coast with a 6th-century monastery on top.
Short Description: Remote 6th-century monastery island, Ireland.
Historical Context: Monks lived here in complete isolation for centuries.
Fun Facts: Featured as Luke Skywalkers hideout in Star Wars: The Force Awakens.

Description: Named after Someshwara (Moon-Lord Shiva), this ghat is visited on Mondays.
Short Description: Monday worship Moon-Shiva ghat.
Historical Context: Shiva as Someshwara is associated with the moon; Mondays are especially sacred here.
Fun Facts: Monday pilgrimages to this ghat are very popular.

Description: One of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines of Lord Shiva, located on the coast of Gujarat.
Short Description: Ancient Jyotirlinga shrine on Gujarat coast.
Historical Context: Destroyed and rebuilt 17 times in history. The current temple was built in 1951.
Fun Facts: Located on the westernmost tip of India where three rivers meet the Arabian Sea.

Description: South Georgia is a remote island in the southern Atlantic Ocean, famous for its extraordinary wildlife — millions of penguins, elephant seals, and albatrosses. It is where Ernest Shackleton is buried after his legendary Antarctic survival story.
Short Description: Remote sub-Antarctic island — extraordinary penguin and seal colonies, Shackleton's burial place.
Historical Context: South Georgia was discovered by the explorer James Cook in 1775. Ernest Shackleton made his legendary 1,300 km open-boat journey from Elephant Island to South Georgia in 1916 to save his crew. He is buried at Grytviken on the island.
Fun Facts:
Ernest Shackleton is buried at Grytviken on the island.
500,000 king penguins breed on the island each year.
The entire island was devastated by whaling in the 19th century — now recovered.

Description: Spiti Valley is a cold desert mountain valley in the Himalayas of Himachal Pradesh, one of India's most remote and stunning landscapes. At 3,800 metres altitude, it features ancient monasteries, dramatic cliffs, and an other-worldly moonscape.
Short Description: India's most dramatic high-altitude cold desert — ancient monasteries and moonscapes.
Ticket Price: Free (Inner Line Permit for some areas)
Historical Context: Spiti means 'the middle land' — between India and Tibet. Key Monastery in Spiti is one of the oldest and largest gompas in the Himalayas. The fossil site at Langza contains 550-million-year-old marine fossils at 4,400 metres — proving the Himalayas were once underwater.
Fun Facts:
Langza has 550-million-year-old marine fossils at 4,400 metres altitude.
Key Monastery is one of India's oldest and most spectacular monasteries.
Spiti gets virtually no rain — India's closest equivalent to the Tibetan plateau.

Description: The state museum of Madhya Pradesh housing prehistoric and medieval artifacts.
Short Description: MP state prehistoric to medieval artifact museum.
Historical Context: Established during British rule; houses artifacts from the Stone Age to the medieval period.
Fun Facts: Has one of the finest collections of medieval MP sculpture.

Monument Name: Statue of Liberty New York USA
Description: The Statue of Liberty is a colossal neoclassical sculpture on Liberty Island in New York Harbor. A gift from France, it was designed by Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi and dedicated in 1886. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Short Description: UNESCO icon of freedom — France's gift to America in New York Harbor.
Historical Context: The statue was designed by Frédéric Bartholdi with its iron framework engineered by Gustave Eiffel (who later built the Eiffel Tower). The statue was shipped to America in 350 pieces and took 4 months to assemble. The torch has been closed to visitors since 1916.
Fun Facts:
The statue's crown has 7 rays — representing the 7 seas and 7 continents.
The torch has been closed to visitors since a sabotage incident in 1916.
Millions of immigrants saw this as their first sight of America.

Description: Stonehenge is a prehistoric stone circle monument, cemetery, and sacred site located in Wiltshire, England. It consists of a ring of standing stones, each around 4 metres high and 2.1 metres wide, weighing around 25 tons.
Short Description: Mysterious prehistoric stone circle monument on Salisbury Plain, England.
Historical Context: Stonehenge was built in several stages between 3000 and 2000 BCE. Its exact purpose remains a mystery, though it is believed to have been a burial ground, a place of healing, or an astronomical observatory. It was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986.
Fun Facts:
The massive sarsen stones came from 25 miles away in Marlborough Downs.
The smaller bluestones were transported from Wales, over 150 miles away.
During the summer solstice, the sun rises in perfect alignment with the central altar stone.

Description: A 13th-century CE Sun Temple at Konark in Odisha, India, and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Short Description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and a 13th-century Sun Temple
Historical Context: The temple was built by King Narasimhadeva I of the Eastern Ganga Dynasty.
Fun Facts:
The temple is shaped like a giant chariot.
It has 12 pairs of intricately carved wheels

Description: Sundarbans is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and the world's largest mangrove forest, shared between India and Bangladesh. It is the home of the Royal Bengal Tiger — the world's only tiger population adapted to living in saline mangrove swamps.
Short Description: World's largest mangrove forest — only tigers that swim in saltwater.
Historical Context: The Sundarbans covers 10,000 sq km of mangrove forest, rivers, and islands. The Royal Bengal Tigers here have uniquely adapted to swimming in saltwater. The forest is also home to estuarine crocodiles, Irrawaddy dolphins, and hundreds of bird species.
Fun Facts:
The tigers here swim between islands in saltwater — unique globally.
The forest covers 10,000 sq km across India and Bangladesh.
The mangrove roots form an extraordinary protective coastal barrier.

Description: The Swiss Alps around Interlaken offer some of the world's most spectacular mountain scenery. Jungfraujoch — Top of Europe at 3,454 metres — is the highest railway station in Europe and offers breathtaking views.
Short Description: World's most spectacular alpine scenery — Top of Europe at 3,454 metres.
Historical Context: The Jungfraujoch railway was completed in 1912 after 16 years of construction. The mountain railway passes through tunnels carved inside the mountain. The Aletsch Glacier — Europe's largest glacier — is visible from the top.
Fun Facts:
The Jungfraujoch railway is the highest railway in Europe.
The Aletsch Glacier below is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
In summer, the temperature at the top is usually -2°C even when it's 25°C below.

Description: The Sydney Opera House is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the 20th century's most distinctive buildings. Designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon, it opened in 1973 and is Australia's most recognised landmark.
Short Description: UNESCO architectural masterpiece — Australia's most iconic landmark.
Historical Context: The Sydney Opera House was designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon who won an international competition in 1957. Construction took 14 years and cost $102 million (10x the original budget). Utzon famously never saw the completed building — he refused to return to Australia after disputes.
Fun Facts:
Utzon never saw the finished building — he left Australia during construction disputes.
The building has 1,000,000+ roof tiles imported from Sweden.
The concert hall has the world's largest mechanical organ with 10,154 pipes.

Description: A famous flat-topped mountain overlooking Cape Town, South Africa. One of the New Seven Wonders of Nature.
Short Description: New Seven Wonders flat-top Cape Town mountain.
Historical Context: Voted one of the New Seven Wonders of Nature in 2011.
Fun Facts:
Over 2,200 plant species found here, more than in the entire UK.
The tablecloth cloud formation is famous.

Description: A stunning white marble palace floating on Lake Pichola in Udaipur, Rajasthan.
Short Description: Floating marble palace on Lake Pichola, Udaipur.
Historical Context: Built by Maharana Jagat Singh II as a summer palace.
Fun Facts: Featured in James Bond Octopussy. Now a luxury heritage hotel.

Description: The Taj Mahal is a white marble mausoleum in Agra built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, one of the New Seven Wonders, and widely considered the world's greatest monument to love.
Short Description: UNESCO World Heritage and New Seven Wonder — the world's most beautiful building.
Historical Context: Construction of the Taj Mahal began in 1632 and took 21 years and 20,000 workers to complete. Shah Jahan was later imprisoned by his son Aurangzeb and reportedly spent his final years gazing at the Taj from Agra Fort across the river.
Fun Facts:
The Taj changes colour — pinkish at dawn, white in day, golden at sunset.
20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants were used in construction.
Shah Jahan spent his last years imprisoned in Agra Fort gazing at the Taj.

Description: Taj-ul-Masajid is one of the largest mosques in Asia, located in Bhopal. Its name means 'Crown of Mosques'. It was built by the Bhopal State ruler Nawab Shah Jahan Begum in the 19th century.
Short Description: One of Asia's largest mosques — the Crown of Mosques — in Bhopal.
Historical Context: Construction of Taj-ul-Masajid began under Nawab Shah Jahan Begum of Bhopal in 1878 but remained incomplete due to lack of funds. It was finally completed in 1985 with government assistance. It can accommodate 175,000 worshippers at once.
Fun Facts:
It is one of the largest mosques in Asia.
The mosque's twin minarets are 18 storeys tall each.
A three-day annual Ijtema (Islamic conference) is held here.

Description: A Hindu temple in Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu, India. It is a part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site
Short Description: A Hindu temple and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Historical Context: The temple is a fine example of Dravidian architecture
Fun Facts: The temple's vimana (tower) is 66 meters high

Description: An ancient Mesoamerican city near Mexico City with the massive Pyramid of the Sun and Pyramid of the Moon.
Short Description: Ancient Mesoamerican Sun Moon pyramid city.
Historical Context: At its peak around 450 CE, Teotihuacan was one of the largest cities in the world with 200,000 people.
Fun Facts:
The Pyramid of the Sun is one of the world's largest pyramids.
Means 'birthplace of the gods' in Aztec.

Description: Ruins of an ancient Mayan city hidden in the jungle of northern Guatemala.
Short Description: Ancient Mayan city ruins, Guatemalan jungle.
Historical Context: One of the largest Mayan cities with population up to 100,000 at its peak.
Fun Facts: Used as the Rebel Base in Star Wars. Has over 3,000 structures.

Description: Tincha Falls is a beautiful waterfall located on the outskirts of Indore. It is a popular weekend picnic spot for residents of Indore, especially during the monsoon season when water flow is at its peak.
Short Description: Beautiful waterfall on Indore's outskirts — popular monsoon picnic spot.
Historical Context: Tincha Falls cascades over rocky terrain surrounded by dense forest. The falls are formed by a seasonal stream that swells dramatically during monsoon. Local tribal communities have lived in this area for centuries.
Fun Facts:
The falls are best visited between July and October.
The surrounding forest has diverse flora and fauna.
The rocky pools at the base are popular for wading.

Description: Tirumala Venkateswara Temple (Tirupati Balaji) is the world's most visited religious site, receiving 50,000-100,000 pilgrims daily. Located on the Tirumala Hills in Andhra Pradesh, the deity is believed to fulfill all wishes.
Short Description: World's most visited religious site — 100,000 pilgrims visit daily at Tirupati.
Historical Context: Tirupati Balaji is the richest temple in the world with annual donations exceeding ₹3,000 crore. The head-tonsuring tradition (Tonsure Seva) brings in 70,000+ kg of hair annually, which is auctioned globally. The prasadam laddu is famous worldwide.
Fun Facts:
The temple receives USD 400 million in donations annually — world's richest.
70,000 kg of hair is offered and auctioned annually.
The Tirumala hills have 7 peaks — pilgrims climb 3,550 steps.

Description: The Tomb of Sher Shah Suri is the mausoleum of Sher Shah Suri, the founder of the Sur Empire in northern India. Located in Sasaram, Bihar, it is one of the finest examples of Afghan-style architecture in India and a masterpiece of Indo-Islamic architecture.
Short Description: Magnificent mausoleum of Sher Shah Suri, founder of the Sur Empire.
Historical Context: Sher Shah Suri defeated the Mughal Emperor Humayun in 1540 and ruled northern India until 1545. He is credited with building the Grand Trunk Road and introducing administrative reforms. His tomb was built during his lifetime and was completed shortly after his death.
Fun Facts:
The tomb stands on a square platform in the middle of an artificial lake.
It rises to a height of 46 metres.
It is considered the finest specimen of Afghan architecture in India.

Description: Features a Trilochan (three-eyed Shiva) temple and is an important Shaivite pilgrimage site.
Short Description: Three-eyed Shiva Trilochan temple ghat.
Historical Context: Home to the Trilochan temple dedicated to the three-eyed form of Lord Shiva.
Fun Facts: One of the important Shaiva pilgrimage spots in Varanasi.

Description: A sacred ghat with a Tripura Bhairavi temple, frequented by tantric practitioners.
Short Description: Tantric goddess Tripura Bhairavi ghat.
Historical Context: Associated with Goddess Tripura Bhairavi, one of the Mahavidyas in Tantric tradition.
Fun Facts: Important site for Shakti worship in Varanasi.

Description: Triveni Sangam in Ujjain is the confluence of the Shipra, Gandharvi, and Saraswati rivers. It is considered a highly sacred site and is one of the major bathing ghats during the Kumbh Mela.
Short Description: Sacred confluence of three rivers — major bathing site during Kumbh Mela.
Historical Context: The Triveni Sangam is considered one of the most sacred bathing spots in Ujjain. The confluence of three rivers — the visible Shipra and the believed-underground Gandharvi and Saraswati — makes it especially holy. Ashes of the deceased are immersed here.
Fun Facts:
The confluence of three rivers makes this especially holy for Hindus.
Ancestral rites (Pind Daan) are performed here by pilgrims.
Boat rides at the Sangam are a peaceful experience.

Description: Named after the saint-poet Tulsidas who wrote the Ramcharitmanas. A historically and spiritually significant ghat.
Short Description: Ghat of saint-poet Tulsidas.
Historical Context: Tulsidas lived and wrote the Ramcharitmanas near this ghat in the 16th century.
Fun Facts: Annual Tulsi festival celebrated here with great enthusiasm.

Description: The ghats of Ujjain along the sacred Shipra River are considered among the most holy in India. The 23 ghats host religious rituals, the Kumbh Mela bathing, and the famous evening Shipra Aarti.
Short Description: 23 sacred ghats along the holy Shipra River — site of Kumbh Mela bathing.
Historical Context: The Shipra River (also called Kshipra) is one of the seven sacred rivers of Hinduism. The 23 ghats on its banks in Ujjain are used for religious bathing throughout the year. During Simhastha Kumbh Mela, millions gather for holy dips.
Fun Facts:
23 ghats line the Shipra River within Ujjain city.
The evening aarti at Ram Ghat is a spectacular spiritual experience.
Simhastha Kumbh Mela sees millions bathing simultaneously here.

Description: The iconic sacred sandstone monolith in Australia's Red Centre, sacred to the Anangu Aboriginal people.
Short Description: Sacred Aboriginal sandstone monolith Australia.
Historical Context: Sacred to the Anangu people for over 30,000 years; climbing Uluru is now banned out of respect.
Fun Facts:
Changes color dramatically at sunrise and sunset.
Rises 348 meters above the desert plain.

Description: Upper Lake (Bada Talab) is one of the oldest man-made lakes in India, built in the 11th century by Raja Bhoj. It is Asia's largest artificial lake and the lifeline of Bhopal city.
Short Description: Asia's largest artificial lake built in the 11th century by Raja Bhoj.
Historical Context: Upper Lake was built by the Paramara dynasty king Raja Bhoj to control flooding and provide water supply to his capital. It covers an area of 361 sq km and has been declared a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance.
Fun Facts:
It is one of Asia's largest man-made lakes.
The lake is a Ramsar Wetland and bird sanctuary.
The Bhopal Gas Tragedy site is located near the lake's southern shore.

Description: Vaishno Devi is one of the most sacred Hindu pilgrimages, dedicated to Goddess Vaishno Devi enshrined in a natural cave in the Trikuta Mountains of Jammu. It receives over 8 million pilgrims annually.
Short Description: India's most visited pilgrimage — sacred cave temple in Trikuta Mountains of Jammu.
Historical Context: The Vaishno Devi cave is a natural cave at 5,200 feet altitude containing three natural rock formations (Pindis) representing the goddess. The 14 km trek from Katra to the cave is one of India's most popular pilgrimage routes.
Fun Facts:
Over 8 million pilgrims visit annually — second only to Tirupati.
The cave has three naturally formed rock idols (Pindis).
The Trek from Katra to the shrine is 14 km one way.

Description: Valley of Flowers is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Uttarakhand, a high-altitude Himalayan valley blanketed with hundreds of species of wildflowers during the monsoon. It is accessible only from July to September.
Short Description: UNESCO Himalayan valley — hundreds of wildflower species bloom in monsoon.
Historical Context: Valley of Flowers was discovered by mountaineer Frank Smythe in 1931 after he accidentally stumbled upon it while returning from the Kamet expedition. The valley is home to rare Himalayan wildlife including snow leopard, brown bear, and blue sheep.
Fun Facts:
Over 600 species of wildflowers bloom in the valley.
It was discovered by British mountaineer Frank Smythe in 1931.
The valley is a UNESCO World Heritage Site along with Nanda Devi National Park.

Description: A national park on the banks of Upper Lake in Bhopal, functioning as both a zoo and a national park.
Short Description: National park-zoo on Upper Lake banks.
Historical Context: Declared a national park in 1979; home to tigers, leopards, bears, and gharials.
Fun Facts:
You can see white tigers here.
Boardwalk along the lake offers spectacular views.

Description: Varanaprastha Ghat is one of the ancient ghats of Varanasi located at the confluence of the Varana and Ganges rivers. This northern end of the Varanasi ghat complex is historically significant.
Short Description: Ancient ghat at the confluence of Varana and Ganges rivers — northern end of ghat complex.
Historical Context: The ghat marks the northern boundary of the sacred Varanasi ghat complex. The Varana River joining the Ganges nearby makes this a Sangam (confluence) — considered doubly sacred in Hinduism. The area is quieter than the central ghats.
Fun Facts:
The ghat marks the northern limit of Varanasi's sacred ghat complex.
The Varana-Ganga confluence nearby is a sacred Sangam.
The area is peaceful and less crowded than southern ghats.

Description: Vatican City is the world's smallest sovereign state, covering just 44 hectares. It is the headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church and home to St. Peter's Basilica, the Sistine Chapel (painted by Michelangelo), and the Vatican Museums.
Short Description: World's smallest state — Sistine Chapel, St. Peter's Basilica, and Vatican Museums.
Historical Context: Vatican City became an independent state in 1929 through the Lateran Treaty with Mussolini's Italy. Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling lying on scaffolding between 1508-1512. The ceiling took 4 years to complete.
Fun Facts:
Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling lying on scaffolding.
The Vatican has its own bank, post office, railway station, and radio station.
The Swiss Guards have protected the Pope since 1506.

Description: Vedh Shala (Jantar Mantar) is an ancient astronomical observatory in Ujjain, one of five built by Maharaja Jai Singh II in the 18th century. Ujjain was the prime meridian of ancient Indian astronomy.
Short Description: Ancient astronomical observatory — Ujjain was once India's prime meridian.
Historical Context: Ujjain was the Greenwich of ancient India — the prime meridian for Indian standard time calculations. The Vedh Shala (Jantar Mantar) was built by Maharaja Jai Singh II of Jaipur in 1719 to observe astronomical phenomena and calculate time.
Fun Facts:
Ujjain's longitude was the prime meridian of ancient Indian astronomy.
The observatory can measure solar time accurate to the minute.
This is one of five Jantar Mantars built by Maharaja Jai Singh II.

Description: Angel Falls (Salto Ángel) is the world's highest uninterrupted waterfall, with a height of 979 metres and a plunge of 807 metres, located in the Canaima National Park in Venezuela. It is named after American aviator Jimmie Angel who was the first to fly over the falls.
Short Description: World's highest uninterrupted waterfall located in Venezuela.
Historical Context: Though known to indigenous Pemon people for centuries, Angel Falls was first recorded in modern times by Ernesto Sanchez La Cruz in 1910. American aviator Jimmie Angel flew over it in 1933 and it was named in his honour. The falls are in the UNESCO World Heritage-listed Canaima National Park.
Fun Facts:
The water falls so far it turns to mist before reaching the base.
The falls drop off the Auyán-tepui mountain.
The indigenous name for the falls is Kerepakupai Merú.

Description: The magnificent royal palace of Versailles, former residence of French monarchs near Paris.
Short Description: Grand royal palace near Paris, France.
Historical Context: Built by Louis XIV. Site of the Treaty of Versailles signed after WWI.
Fun Facts: Has 2,300 rooms and 800 hectares of gardens. UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Description: Victoria Falls is the world's largest sheet of falling water, straddling the border of Zimbabwe and Zambia. The local name 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' means 'The Smoke that Thunders' — the spray is visible 50 km away.
Short Description: World's largest waterfall sheet — 'The Smoke that Thunders' on Zambia-Zimbabwe border.
Historical Context: Victoria Falls was named by David Livingstone in 1855 after Queen Victoria. It is the only waterfall in the world designated as both a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a Wonder of the World. In full flow, 625 million litres of water per minute cascade over the 1.7 km wide falls.
Fun Facts:
The spray from the falls can be seen from 50 km away.
Devil's Pool — a natural infinity pool at the falls' edge — is swimmable in dry season.
The falls are wider than Niagara and taller than Iguazu.

Description: A large marble building in Kolkata, West Bengal, India. It was built between 1906 and 1921
Short Description: A large marble building and a museum
Historical Context: The memorial was built in memory of Queen Victoria.
Fun Facts: The memorial is located in a beautiful garden.

Description: Built by the royal family of Vijayanagram, South India, featuring South Indian architectural elements.
Short Description: South Indian Vijayanagram royal ghat.
Historical Context: Constructed by the maharajas of Vijayanagaram from Andhra Pradesh.
Fun Facts: Example of South Indian royalty's connection to Varanasi.

Description: The Vishnupad Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Vishnu, located in Gaya, Bihar, India. The temple is believed to be built on the footprint of Lord Vishnu, and is one of the most sacred pilgrimage sites for Hindus performing the Pind Daan ritual.
Short Description: Sacred Hindu temple built over the mythical footprint of Lord Vishnu.
Historical Context: The current Vishnupad Temple was built in 1787 by Maharani Ahilyabai Holkar of Indore. The temple enshrines a 40 cm long footprint (pada) of Lord Vishnu embedded in solid rock, said to be the spot where Vishnu placed his foot on the demon Gayasur.
Fun Facts:
The temple's main tower (shikhara) is 30 metres high.
Gaya is one of the most sacred cities for performing ancestral rites (Shraddha).
The footprint impression is set within an octagonal basin of basalt rock.

Description: The Vishwa Shanti Stupa (World Peace Pagoda) is a Buddhist white-domed stupa on the Ratnagiri Hill in Rajgir, Bihar, India. It was built by the Japanese Buddhist organisation Nipponzan-Myohoji and is dedicated to world peace.
Short Description: World Peace Pagoda built on Ratnagiri Hill in Rajgir, Bihar.
Historical Context: The stupa was constructed by the Japanese Buddhist monk Nichidatsu Fujii and the Nipponzan-Myohoji order, dedicated to world peace after the devastation of World War II. Rajgir is one of the most sacred sites in Buddhism, as Buddha spent several months here delivering sermons.
Fun Facts:
It can be reached by a ropeway (cable car) from the base of the hill.
The stupa houses four golden statues of the Buddha.
Rajgir was the first capital of the Magadha Empire.

Description: The Weddell Sea in Antarctica contains some of the clearest water in the world and is home to extraordinary wildlife including emperor penguins, Weddell seals, and orca whales. It is one of the most remote and pristine marine environments on Earth.
Short Description: World's clearest water — emperor penguins and orcas in pristine Antarctic waters.
Historical Context: The Weddell Sea has some of the clearest water on Earth with visibility up to 79 metres. It is home to the world's largest emperor penguin colonies and is the habitat of Weddell seals which can dive to 600 metres depth.
Fun Facts:
Weddell Sea has the clearest water on Earth — 79 m visibility.
Emperor penguins breed here in temperatures of -40°C.
Ernest Shackleton's ship Endurance was crushed by Weddell Sea ice in 1915.

Description: Stunning tropical islands in Queensland's Great Barrier Reef region with Whitehaven Beach.
Short Description: Tropical Queensland Great Barrier Reef Whitehaven Beach.
Historical Context: The islands sit in the heart of the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park.
Fun Facts:
Whitehaven Beach has 98% pure silica sand.
The islands were named by Captain Cook as he passed on Whit Sunday 1770.

Description: Yellowstone is the world's first national park, established in 1872. It sits on the Yellowstone Caldera — a supervolcano — and features the world's largest collection of geothermal features including Old Faithful geyser.
Short Description: World's first national park — supervolcano with Old Faithful geyser.
Historical Context: Yellowstone sits on one of Earth's largest volcanic systems — an eruption would affect the entire Northern Hemisphere. The park has over 10,000 geothermal features — more than anywhere else on Earth. Old Faithful erupts roughly every 90 minutes.
Fun Facts:
Yellowstone has more geysers than all other countries combined.
Old Faithful geyser erupts every 60-110 minutes.
The park is home to the largest bison population in North America.

Description: Yosemite National Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site in California's Sierra Nevada. It features El Capitan, Half Dome, giant sequoias, and spectacular waterfalls. It was instrumental in inspiring the US national park movement.
Short Description: UNESCO Heritage park — El Capitan, Half Dome, and giant sequoias of California.
Historical Context: Yosemite was championed by John Muir and became a national park in 1890. El Capitan is the world's largest exposed granite monolith. The park's giant sequoias are the world's largest trees by volume — some over 3,000 years old.
Fun Facts:
El Capitan is the world's largest exposed granite monolith.
Yosemite Falls is the tallest waterfall in North America at 739 metres.
Ansel Adams' photographs of Yosemite are among the most famous in history.

Description: A surreal forest of towering sandstone pillars in Hunan Province, China.
Short Description: Avatar-like sandstone pillar forest, China.
Historical Context: China's first national forest park, established in 1982.
Fun Facts: Inspired the floating Hallelujah Mountains in Avatar. Over 3,000 sandstone pillars.

Description: Zion National Park in Utah features stunning red rock canyons, the famous Angels Landing hike, and the Narrows slot canyon. It is one of the most visited national parks in the USA with 4.5 million visitors annually.
Short Description: Stunning red rock canyons and Angels Landing — Utah's most popular national park.
Historical Context: Zion was established as a national park in 1919. Angels Landing — a 454-metre rock fin with chain handholds — is one of the world's most thrilling hikes. The Narrows is a gorge walk through the Virgin River between sheer 300-metre walls.
Fun Facts:
Angels Landing has chain handholds for the final 400-metre scramble.
The Narrows canyon walls are only 6 metres apart in some sections.
The park sees more than 4.5 million visitors annually.
